content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

11 min read
5 hours ago

--

I used to write the whole SCRUB discipline into every prompt, every time. Then I built a repo where the discipline lives in the codebase instead — a CLAUDE.md constitution, path-scoped rules, on-demand skills, read-only subagents, and hooks that fire no matter what the agent decides. It's public. You can clone it and read every piece.
There’s a repo on my GitHub called ClaudeBox, and its tagline is the most honest thing on the whole account: “a sandbox where Claude Code does the heavy lifting and I take the credit.” Inside it is RecipeBox — a full-stack recipe app built with Aspire, ASP.NET Core, and Angular, orchestrated entirely on your laptop with no cloud spend. Boot it with aspire run and the dashboard comes up with logs, traces, and health for every resource.
But the app isn’t the point. Or rather — it’s exactly half the point. The other half is the .claude/ folder sitting next to it, because that folder is the record of a shift I've been making in how I work: I stopped writing prompts and started engineering Claude.
Let me explain what that sentence actually means, because it’s not a cute reframe. It’s a different job.
First, the honest part
RecipeBox is a scaffold. It’s a genuinely good app — layered, tested, properly wired — but it’s built to be cloned and continued, with deliberate seams where the exercises begin. The README says, in as many words, to verify the exact Aspire commands and API names against the docs, because Aspire ships fast and some surface changes occur between versions. I’d rather label a real 90% honestly than sell you a polished 100% that doesn’t survive git clone.
I build these on purpose. “I do disciplined AI-assisted development” is a claim, and claims are free. A repo you can clone, read, and run is a different kind of evidence — it shows the process, the guardrails, the trade-offs, and the seams. If you’re evaluating whether I actually know how to drive an agent through a real .NET stack, that folder will tell you more in ten minutes than this entire post will. Go read it. Then come back, and I’ll tell you what it’s doing.
The old way: SCRUB as a tax you pay every prompt
If you’ve read anything else I’ve written, you know SCRUB. It’s my five-part discipline for AI-assisted coding — Scope, Constraints, Restrictions, Usage, Behavior — and the idea is to apply all five to every prompt, every time. Each element names exactly which class of bug you invite when you skip it. Skip Scope, and the AI builds the wrong thing. Skip Constraints, and it builds the right thing in the wrong patterns. Skip Restrictions — the star of the framework — and you get the Plausibility Trap: code that compiles, looks idiomatic, passes its tests, and is quietly wrong in a way only domain knowledge catches.
SCRUB works. I’ll defend it on,” “thin controllers, DTOs at the boundary,restating your entire architecture from memory into a chat box a whiteboard all day. But here’s the thing nobody puts on the slide: writing all five elements into every prompt is a tax. By the twentieth prompt of a build, you are typing “Do NOT hardcode connection strings” and “thin controllers, DTOs at the boundary” and “plan before you touch more than one file” for the twentieth time. You are re-stating your entire architecture, from memory, into a chat box, at the exact moment your attention should be on the actual problem.
And the tax has a nasty failure mode. The one time you’re tired, or in a hurry, or halfway through a debugging spiral at 6 pm, you skip the Restrictions block “just this once” — and that’s the prompt that commits a secret or oversells the last unit or defaults the tip to 20%. The discipline is only as good as your worst moment of remembering to apply it.
So I asked the obvious question. If SCRUB is five elements I apply every single time — what if they didn’t have to live in the prompt at all?
The shift: every SCRUB element has a home in the repo
Here’s the move, and it’s the whole post in one table. Each of the five SCRUB elements has a natural home somewhere in a Claude Code project — a place where it can live once, load itself when relevant, and stop being something I retype:
SCRUB element Where it lives now What it becomes Scope a skill in .claude/skills/ a procedure the agent follows when a task matches Constraints a rule in .claude/rules/ + CLAUDE.md conventions that auto-load by file path Restrictions a hook in .claude/hooks/ a guardrail that fires no matter what the agent decides Usage CLAUDE.md (the constitution) the operating context, read every session Behavior CLAUDE.md + subagents how work proceeds; what gets delegated read-only
The .claude/README.md in the repo states the same idea as a rule of thumb, and it's worth memorizing: a rule (or CLAUDE.md) is something Claude should know; a skill is a procedure Claude should follow when a task matches; a subagent is work Claude should delegate to keep the main context clean; and a hook is something that must happen no matter what Claude decides.
Read that last one twice. “No matter what Claude decides.” That is a Restriction that has stopped being a polite request in a prompt and become a physical property of the repository. That’s the difference between asking and enforcing — and it’s the whole reason this shift is worth making.
Let me walk each home with the actual artifacts.
CLAUDE.md is a SCRUB prompt wearing a suit
Open the CLAUDE.md at the root of RecipeBox, and here is its opening line, verbatim: project memory, written as a SCRUB prompt — Scope, Constraints, Restrictions, Usage, Behavior. Loaded every session.
The file has those exact five headers. The constitution of the entire project is a SCRUB prompt — one I wrote once, that Claude Code auto-discovers by walking up from the working directory, and that survives a /compact. The Scope section says what's in bounds and, more importantly, what's out of bounds (don't build i18n unprompted). The Constraints section pins the stack — Aspire 13 on .NET 10, ASP.NET Core, EF Core on Npgsql, Angular standalone components — and the canonical commands to use verbatim. The Restrictions section says don't hardcode connection strings, don't put business logic in the AppHost, don't run ng serve by hand. Usage describes the world (everything's local, services find each other through discovery). Behavior sets the working rhythm: plan before touching more than one file, wait for approval, use the matching skill instead of freelancing.
The payoff is a line I’m quietly proud of: because every rule has one obvious home under one of five headers, every misstep is diagnosable by section. When the agent does something wrong, I don’t wonder what went wrong in the ether — I ask which SCRUB element failed, and the fix goes back into that section. The prompt framework became the debugging framework. That’s not a coincidence I planned; it’s what happens when your discipline has structure.
Constraints that load themselves: rules
Typing your architecture conventions into every prompt is the single most repetitive part of the old tax. So they moved into .claude/rules/, and the trick is that rules are path-scoped. backend.md carries a little header declaring it applies to src/RecipeBox.ApiService/**. It loads into context only when Claude touches the API — thin controllers, DTOs at the boundary, DbContext via the Aspire integration, async all the way down, validate at the edge. frontend.md loads only for the Angular app. aspire.md loads only for the AppHost.
This is the Constraints element of SCRUB, except it now costs me zero keystrokes and — better — it can’t bleed. The backend conventions don’t clutter the context when Claude is writing an Angular component. The right rules show up for the right files, automatically, and the prompt that used to carry three paragraphs of “here’s how my codebase works” carries none.
Scope frozen into a procedure: skills
The add-endpoint skill is my favorite artifact in the repo because it's a whole SCRUB prompt frozen solid into a reusable procedure. RecipeBox has nine skills in .claude/skills/ (three I authored — add-endpoint, new-component, add-aspire-resource — plus six vendored Aspire and Playwright ones kept as-shipped so they can be re-vendored). But add-endpoint is the one that fully illustrates the idea.
It encodes the entire layered architecture of the API as a step-by-step playbook: controller → facade → business → data layer → repository, each with strict, non-negotiable responsibilities. The controller does HTTP only. The facade owns validation and caching. The business layer translates ViewModel to domain, applies domain rules, and maps domain back to a ServiceModel. The data layer composes repository calls into whole operations and owns the transaction boundary. The repository does EF queries and nothing else. There’s a checklist at the bottom — sixteen boxes — that the agent ticks before it calls the work done.
That skill is Scope, Constraints, Restrictions, and Behavior all at once, written once, that I never type again. When I say “add an endpoint to list recipes by category,” the skill loads on its own and the agent builds it my way, down to which layer is allowed to touch the DbContext. The scrub-prompts doc in the repo says it best: when you reuse a prompt two or three times, promote it to a skill so you stop needing the prompt at all. That's the asymptote this whole approach bends toward — the best SCRUB prompt is the one you never have to write, because its scope already lives in a skill.
Restrictions that can’t be forgotten: hooks
This is where the shift stops being a convenience and starts being a genuinely different guarantee.
In the old model, “Do NOT commit secrets” is a line in a Restrictions block — a request the agent honors if you remembered to write it and it remembered to obey. In RecipeBox, that Restriction is a file: .claude/hooks/secret-guard.sh, wired as a PreToolUse hook. Before any edit or write lands, the hook scans the payload for anything credential-shaped — an API key pattern, an AWS access-key ID, a PEM private-key header, a password literal, a Postgres or Redis URL with inline credentials — and if it matches, it exits with code 2. Exit 2 means deny. The edit does not happen. The message it prints back is a small act of teaching: use Aspire-injected config, not literals.
Read what that changes. The secret guard doesn’t ask Claude to be careful. It doesn’t depend on my worst-moment discipline or the agent’s mood. It fires no matter what the agent decides, on every single edit, deterministically, forever. It is the Restrictions element of SCRUB — the star of the framework — turned from a sentence into a law of physics for this repo. The tired-developer-at-6 pm failure mode I described earlier? The one prompt where you skip the guardrail “just this once”? It can’t happen here because the guardrail isn’t in the prompt anymore.
There’s a second hook, format.sh, wired as PostToolUse: after every edit, it formats the file — dotnet format for C#, the Angular app's own pinned Prettier for the frontend — and it's written carefully to never block an edit even if the formatter is missing. That's the Behavior element made automatic. Consistency stops being something I nag about in review and becomes something that already happened before I looked.
Behavior you delegate: subagents
The last home is for the work you want done without polluting your main context. RecipeBox has four read-only subagents in .claude/agents/: a code-reviewer that checks a diff against the repo's conventions and reports blockers, suggestions, and nits; a test-gap-analyzer; an api-contract-checker that hunts for drift between the API's boundary types and the Angular models that mirror them; and a skills-evals agent that audits whether generated code actually followed the skills.
The critical design choice: they are all read-only. Their frontmatter grants Read, Grep, Glo
